subject: Conditional Probability and Independence
question: Suppose 5 cards are drawn from a deck of 52 playing cards. What is the probability that all cards are the same suit, i.e. a flush?
explanation: We must find $\Pr(\text{a flush}) = \Pr(\text{5 spades}) + \Pr(\text{5 hearts}) + \Pr(\text{5 diamonds}) + \Pr(\text{5 clubs})$ Now, the probability of getting 5 spades is found as follows: $\Pr(\text{5 spades}) = \Pr(\text{1st card is a spade})\Pr(\text{2nd card is a spade} \mid \text{1st card is a spade}) \times \ldots \times \Pr(\text{5th card is a spade} \mid \text{1st,2nd,3rd,4th cards are spades}) = \frac{13}{52} \times \frac{12}{51} \times \frac{11}{50} \times \frac{10}{49} \times \frac{9}{48}$ Since the above calculation is the same for any of the four suits, $\Pr(\text{a flush}) = 4 \times \frac{13}{52} \times \frac{12}{51} \times \frac{11}{50} \times \frac{10}{49} \times \frac{9}{48}$ We end this section by showing that $\Pr(* \mid A)$ satisfies the properties of ordinary probabilities. Theorem 9.2 The function $B \to \Pr(B \mid A)$ defines a probability measure. Proof. 1. Since $0 \le \Pr(A \cap B) \le \Pr(A)$, $0 \le \Pr(B \mid A) \le 1$. 2. $\Pr(S \mid A) = \frac{\Pr(S \cap A)}{\Pr(A)} = \frac{\Pr(A)}{\Pr(A)} = 1$. 3. Suppose that $B_1, B_2, \ldots,$ are mutually exclusive events. Then $B_1 \cap A, B_2 \cap A, \ldots,$ are mutually exclusive. Thus, $\Pr\left(\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} B_n \mid A\right) = \frac{\Pr\left(\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} (B_n \cap A)\right)}{\Pr(A)} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \Pr(B_n \cap A)}{\Pr(A)} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \Pr(B_n \mid A)$ Thus, every theorem we have proved for an ordinary probability function holds for a conditional probability function. For example, we have $\Pr(B^c \mid A) = 1 - \Pr(B \mid A)$. Prior and Posterior Probabilities The probability $\Pr(A)$ is the probability of the event $A$ prior to introducing new events that might affect $A$. It is known as the prior probability of $A$. When the occurrence of an event $B$ will affect the event $A$ then $\Pr(A \mid B)$ is known as the posterior probability of $A$.
